summary: American artist best known for narrative story quilts combining painted imagery with text. African-American; born 1930 in Harlem, New York. Key works include Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima?, Tar Beach, The French Collection (including Picasso's Studio and Dancing at the Louvre), and the American People Series of paintings. Also known for her Witch and Family of Women mask sculptures. Inspired to work in the quilt medium after visiting the Rijksmuseum and seeing Nepali thangkas.

works.name: Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima? (1983)
works.description: Ringgold's first and most famous story quilt. 56 squares; a narrative about a Harlem restaurateur named Willa Marie Simone (reimagining the "Aunt Jemima" stereotype) and her husband Big Rufus. The outer ring of 26 portraits has brown/yellow backgrounds; inner ring has yellow. The story spans 7 of the 56 squares. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Ringgold's first story quilt; reimagines Aunt Jemima as a successful Harlem restaurateur named Willa Marie Simone with husband Big Rufus
examples: In an artwork whose title begins with these three words, a woman opens a Harlem restaurant with her husband, Big Rufus.
This artist depicted the story of the title character becoming a successful businesswoman in Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima?

clues.clue: 26 portraits with brown and yellow backgrounds surround narrative text squares; partially inspired by the artist's experience with overweight stereotypes
examples: Twenty-six portraits with yellow and brown backgrounds surround a script in a work in this medium that was partially inspired by its artist's experience with overweight stereotypes.

clues.clue: The quilt has 56 total squares, with the Aunt Jemima storyline across 7 of them
examples: This woman and her husband Big Rufus open a Harlem restaurant in a story told across 7 of the 56 squares of a 1983 artwork.

works.name: Tar Beach / Tar Beach #2
works.description: Story quilt (and children's book) in which a girl named Cassie Louise Lightfoot imagines flying over the George Washington Bridge while her family dines on their Harlem rooftop at night. Tar Beach #2 is the quilt version made from fabric. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A girl named Cassie Louise Lightfoot flies through the night sky above the George Washington Bridge while a family dines on a rooftop
examples: This artist showed Cassie Louise Lightfoot soaring through the night sky above the George Washington Bridge in the background of a work in which a family shares a rooftop dinner.
A two-part work in this medium features a family eating dinner at night on an apartment building's rooftop as a young girl flies over the George Washington Bridge.

clues.clue: Tar Beach inspired by African American folktales about flying; also inspired Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon
examples: Folktales about this activity inspired Faith Ringgold's Tar Beach and Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon.

clues.clue: Tar Beach #2 is made from fabric; it is part of the same diptych as Tar Beach
examples: This material is used to depict a girl flying over the George Washington Bridge in Tar Beach 2.

works.name: The French Collection, Part I (including Dancing at the Louvre and Picasso's Studio)
works.description: A series of story quilts set in Paris featuring a recurring character named Willa Marie Simone. Dancing at the Louvre shows a black woman and her daughters leaping in front of the Mona Lisa. Picasso's Studio shows Willa Marie posing as a model for Les Demoiselles d'Avignon. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Picasso's Studio (part of French Collection, Part I) depicts Willa Marie Simone posing as a model for Les Demoiselles d'Avignon
examples: The same woman, Willa Marie Simone, poses as one of the models for Les Demoiselles d'Avignon in Picasso's Studio, part of this artist's The French Collection, Part I.
Picasso can be seen painting Les Demoiselles d'Avignon in this artist's French Collection.

clues.clue: Dancing at the Louvre depicts a black woman and her daughters leaping/dancing in front of the Mona Lisa
examples: This artist of Tar Beach depicted a black woman and her daughters leaping in front of the Mona Lisa in Dancing at the Louvre.
A child in a pink dress jumps in front of the Mona Lisa in one of these [quilt] works.

works.name: American People Series / Flag Paintings
works.description: A series of paintings created in response to the 1967 U.S. race riots. The 20th work, subtitled 'Die,' shows a bloody scene of white and Black figures in chaos, inspired by Picasso's Guernica. Other flag paintings include The Flag Is Bleeding (black man and white couple with Stars and Stripes) and a 'Die' flag painting that replaces stripes with the N-word (in the Black Light series). Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Die' (20th work in American People Series) — bloody scene with white boy and black girl at center, cocktail dresses, suits, guns; inspired by Picasso's Guernica; painted in response to 1967 race riots
examples: The 20th work in this artist's 'American People' Series, subtitled 'Die,' places a huddled white boy and black girl in the center of a bloody mess of cocktail dresses, suits, guns, and chaos.
Ringgold's 'American People' was inspired by the depictions of chaos in this artist's Guernica.

clues.clue: The Flag Is Bleeding — black man and white couple interwoven with Stars and Stripes; inspired by Jasper Johns's flag paintings
examples: The Stars and Stripes are interwoven with the images of a black man and a white couple in a painting by this artist called The Flag Is Bleeding.
Ringgold was inspired by Jasper Johns for her The American Flag is Bleeding.

clues.clue: Flag painting in Black Light series: the word 'Die' over the stars, N-word replaces stripes
examples: Another painting of the American flag by this artist shows the word 'Die' over the stars and replaces the stripes with the N-word.

works.name: Witch Series / Family of Women Masks
works.description: Ringgold's mask and sculpture series. The Witch series incorporates raffia as decorative hair; Women's Liberation Talking Mask is a key piece. The Family of Woman Mask series includes a life-sized statue of Wilt Chamberlain. The Family of Women series adorned masks with garments. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Witch series uses raffia as decorative hair; includes Women's Liberation Talking Mask; Family of Woman Mask series includes life-sized Wilt Chamberlain statue
examples: This artist's Witch series uses raffia as decorative hair for many of its pieces, such as Women's Liberation Talking Mask. This artist's similar Family of Woman Mask series includes a life-sized statue of Wilt Chamberlain.

clues.clue: Family of Women series: masks adorned with garments
examples: Ringgold adorned these objects with garments in her Family of Women series.

works.name: Change 2 / General Story Quilts and Medium
works.description: Change 2 is an early work depicting a black woman in a pink swimsuit with a dark shadow, subtitled 'Over 100 Pound Weight Loss Performance.' Ringgold was inspired to experiment with quilts after visiting the Rijksmuseum and seeing Nepali thangkas there. The Street Story Quilt is a triptych with sections titled 'Accident,' 'Fire,' and 'Homecoming.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Inspired to work in the quilt medium after visiting the Rijksmuseum and seeing Nepali thangkas there
examples: This creator of Change 2 was inspired to experiment with that work's medium after visiting the Rijksmuseum and seeing Nepali thangkas there.

clues.clue: Change 2 depicts a black woman in a pink swimsuit with a dark shadow, subtitled 'Over 100 Pound Weight Loss Performance'
examples: A work by this artist depicts a black woman with a pink swimsuit and a dark shadow in its center and is partially subtitled as an 'Over 100 Pound Weight Loss Performance.'

clues.clue: Street Story Quilt is a triptych with sections 'Accident,' 'Fire,' and 'Homecoming'
examples: 'Accident,' 'Fire,' and 'Homecoming' are the three sections of a triptych in this medium. [referring to Ringgold's Street Story Quilt]

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Faith Ringgold is an African-American artist born 1930 in Harlem, New York. Best known for her narrative story quilts, she also created paintings and mask sculptures. Also illustrated a children's book based on Gwendolyn Brooks's poetry. Associated with second-wave feminism and Black arts movements. Her full name is Faith Willi Jones (married name Ringgold).

clues.clue: Known for narrative story quilts; key works include Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima?, Tar Beach, and Aunt Harriet's Underground Railroad in the Sky
examples: For 10 points, name this black artist of narrative quilts like Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima?

clues.clue: Also illustrated a book for Gwendolyn Brooks set in Bronzeville
examples: Faith Ringgold illustrated a book by this author set in a neighborhood that also titled her first poetry collection, A Street in Bronzeville.

clues.clue: Works in a dance among cables of a bridge and children on a rooftop watching a girl fly through the night sky — referring to the Tar Beach series
examples: In one of their works, people dance amongst the cables of a bridge, and in another work from that series two children lie on a blanket on a rooftop and watch while a girl flies through the night sky above.

comprehensive_summary: Faith Ringgold (born 1930, Harlem, New York; full name Faith Willi Jones) is an African-American artist best known for her narrative story quilts that combine painted imagery with written text. Her most famous work is Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima? (1983), her first story quilt, which reimagines the 'Aunt Jemima' mammy stereotype as a successful Harlem restaurateur named Willa Marie Simone operating a restaurant with her husband Big Rufus. The quilt consists of 56 squares, with 26 brown-and-yellow-backgrounded portrait squares surrounding narrative text blocks; it was partially inspired by Ringgold's personal experience with overweight stereotypes. Ringgold was inspired to experiment with the quilt medium after visiting the Rijksmuseum and seeing Nepali thangkas there. Her Tar Beach story quilt depicts a girl named Cassie Louise Lightfoot flying over the George Washington Bridge while her family eats dinner on a Harlem rooftop; the work was inspired by African American folktales about flying (the same tradition that inspired Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon), and was also adapted into a children's book. Tar Beach #2 is the fabric quilt version. The French Collection, Part I is a series of story quilts set in Paris featuring recurring character Willa Marie Simone: Dancing at the Louvre shows the character and her daughters leaping in front of the Mona Lisa, while Picasso's Studio depicts Willa Marie posing as a model for Les Demoiselles d'Avignon. Ringgold's American People Series of paintings was created in response to the 1967 U.S. race riots; its 20th work, Die, depicts a bloody scene with a white boy and black girl at the center amid chaos, inspired by Picasso's Guernica. Her flag paintings include The Flag Is Bleeding (a black man and white couple interwoven with the Stars and Stripes, inspired by Jasper Johns), and a work in the Black Light series replacing the flag's stripes with a racial slur. She also created sculptural mask work: her Witch series uses raffia as hair (including Women's Liberation Talking Mask), and her Family of Woman Mask series includes a life-sized statue of Wilt Chamberlain. Ringgold illustrated a children's book based on Gwendolyn Brooks's A Street in Bronzeville poetry. Her work with the Street Story Quilt, a triptych with sections titled 'Accident,' 'Fire,' and 'Homecoming,' also appears in quizbowl clues.
